data.title: Lument Finance Trust outlines new $450M JPMorgan facility to drive portfolio repositioning
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lument Finance Trust (LFT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Flynn reported that the company is navigating a resilient economic landscape but faces "lingering uncertainty in the near term punctuated by the ongoing geopolitical volatility and the fast-moving trade and tariff policy shifts in the U.S." Flynn highlighted the recent 25-basis point Fed funds rate cut as a "cautionary positive development for multifamily lending" and noted the constructive fundamentals in the multifamily sector, with rent growth described as modest and stable, and occupancy strong.
  • Flynn announced, "Last week, we entered into a new repurchase agreement with JPMorgan, providing the company with up to $450 million in aggregate advances," emphasizing this as a "critical step in repositioning our existing portfolio and subject to market conditions, enabling us to take advantage of new financing opportunities."
  • The near-term focus remains on "driving value through active asset management, resolving legacy positions efficiently, in executing our financing strategy" and redeploying capital into the core lending strategy focused on middle market multifamily.
  • CFO James Briggs stated, "For the third quarter of '25, we reported net income to common stockholders of approximately $700,000 or $0.01 per share. We also reported distributable earnings of approximately $1 million or $0.02 per share." He further highlighted, "Our Q3 net interest income was $5.1 million, a decline from $7 million recorded in Q2."
  • President Greg Calvert noted, "During the third quarter, LFT experienced $49 million of loan payoffs. As of September 30, our total loan portfolio consisted of 51 floating rate loans with an aggregate unpaid principal balance of approximately $840 million."

Outlook

  • Flynn indicated the company intends to "redeploy capital into our core lending strategy focused on middle market multifamily" as market stability returns.
  • Management expressed confidence in the company's "focus, prudence and flexibility" and reiterated that their "managers origination and asset management expertise remain a key differentiator."
  • No explicit forward-looking quantitative guidance was provided.

Financial Results

  • Lument Finance Trust reported net income to common stockholders of $700,000 or $0.01 per share and distributable earnings of $1 million or $0.02 per share for Q3 2025.
  • Net interest income declined to $5.1 million from $7 million in Q2, attributed to a reduction in portfolio balance and principal loan repayments used to pay down secured financings, along with the reversal of certain accrued interest and nonaccrual loans.
  • Total operating expenses were $3.1 million in Q3, down from $3.2 million in Q2.
  • The company recorded a provision for credit losses of approximately $900,000 and charged off $200,000 of reserves for an asset that went REO.
  • Unrestricted cash balance was $56 million at quarter end, with total equity at $230 million and book value of common stock at $3.25 per share, slightly down from $3.27 as of June 30.
  • The portfolio consisted of 51 floating rate loans with an unpaid principal balance of $840 million, and 90% collateralized by multifamily properties.

Q&A

  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: "Do you guys feel that you've identified the bulk of the issues in the portfolio at this point? Or are you still kind of going through things and we could see some further downgrades coming forward?" CEO Flynn: "I think we have a good handle on the portfolio... we feel that we've identified all of the known issues... there's no expectation that there would be further change as we all know, and we look around at the market and things that are going on."
  • Muller: "How are you guys thinking about portfolio growth in the coming quarters? Is the primary focus going to be on asset management? Or could we see some new loans coming on, especially given the new financing?" Flynn: "The new financing certainly gives us more flexibility to add assets... we hope that things remain, and that gives us an opportunity to put more assets on the books in the coming quarters."
  • Muller: "Is there anything you guys can share on timing for expectations for REO sales? And can you just remind me, is there any financing against that REO? Or is it held unlevered?" Flynn: "Today, we've consistently held the REO that we have... unlevered. We do have flexibility to put some financing against the value of those assets... timing for the REOs is very asset specific."
  • Greg Bennett: "Is there any change in your relationship with your sponsor, ORIX USA?" Flynn: "First, no, there's no change in the relationship between ORIX and Lument... I don't think there's any reason to think that the Hilco acquisition would have a material impact and certainly not a negative impact on LFT."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly cautious, focusing questions on credit risk recognition, portfolio growth prospects, REO sales strategy, and sponsor relationships.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing active management, portfolio clarity, and the benefits of new financing. Flynn stated, "we feel that we've identified all of the known issues..."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a shift from a stability-focused discussion to one highlighting portfolio repositioning and new financing flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net income and distributable earnings per share decreased from $0.05 in Q2 to $0.01 and $0.02 in Q3, respectively.
  • Net interest income declined from $7 million to $5.1 million.
  • Dividend per common share was reduced from $0.06 in Q2 to $0.04 in Q3.
  • The portfolio experienced further contraction in loan count and outstanding principal balance.
  • Risk ratings shifted, with 7 loans rated at 5 in Q3 compared to 8 in Q2.
  • Management's tone shifted from emphasizing stability and asset management to highlighting liquidity, capital preservation, and strategic repositioning through the JPMorgan facility.
  • Analysts were more engaged in Q3, posing detailed questions on risk management and growth strategies, while the previous quarter had no questions during the call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "lingering uncertainty in the near term punctuated by the ongoing geopolitical volatility and the fast-moving trade and tariff policy shifts in the U.S.", as well as the economic drag from the recent federal government shutdown.
  • Specific asset-level risks were highlighted, with 7 loans risk-rated 5 and all in monetary or maturity default, representing $86.4 million or approximately 10% of the investment portfolio's unpaid principal balance.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include "actively managing the portfolio," "proactively seeking positive resolutions through modifications, extensions and REO strategies," and maintaining a "conservative liquidity posture."

Final Takeaway

Lument Finance Trust leadership outlined a strategic transition this quarter, marked by the establishment of a new $450 million JPMorgan facility to enhance portfolio repositioning and future growth. While earnings and portfolio metrics saw sequential declines, management maintains that risk issues have been identified and are being actively managed. The focus is now on leveraging increased liquidity for core multifamily lending as the company works through legacy asset resolutions and seeks to capitalize on emerging market opportunities as conditions stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500’s 7,000 mark is ‘in sight’ despite current turbulence – strategist
data.description:
Partners Group’s chief investment strategist Anastasia Amoroso believes the S&P 500 (SP500) is poised to reach the 7,000 mark, driven by seasonal forces and reduced concerns from Washington.

In an interview with CNBC, Amoroso noted that the index is only about 2% away from this milestone and expressed confidence that recent economic developments could propel it forward.

She highlighted that the resolution of the government shutdown would likely boost consumer sentiment, as government workers are expected to receive their back pay around Thanksgiving.

The strategist also addressed upcoming Nvidia (NVDA) earnings, suggesting they could positively influence tech sector (XLK), (IYW), (VGT) sentiment amid continued data center build-out by hyperscalers.

However, she cautioned investors about overreliance on semiconductor stocks (SMH), (SOXX), (XSD). “Do the stocks keep surging four, five years from now? I don’t know. I think investors should diversify away just from semiconductors.”

Regarding the Federal Reserve’s next moves, Amoroso maintained that a December rate cut remains “on the table” despite market odds dropping to around 50-50. She cited concerning labor market deterioration, noting that layoffs are up 44% year over year, and argued that the economy “does need support” at this critical juncture.

When questioned about how rate cuts would help if labor weakness stems from AI implementation, Amoroso explained that lower rates could stimulate other economic sectors.

“All of a sudden you [could] have those cash flows go up because you’re not paying quite as much out in interest expense,” she said, suggesting these savings could fund expansion, capital expenditures, and potentially even hiring.

data.title: U.S. stocks fall as post-shutdown optimism fades, economic anxiety rises
data.description:
Wall Street's major indexes were lower on Thursday, as the U.S. government shutdown came to an end, and investors turned focus to company earnings, as well as jobs and inflation data that should resume.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.2% in midday trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.9%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1%.

Some of the mega-caps leading the decline within the S&P 500 ((SP500)) include Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Tesla (TSLA), and Palantir (PLTR).

The U.S. House of Representatives on Wednesday passed the stopgap spending bill that will end the longest U.S. government shutdown in history, which lasted 43 days. With it, President Donald Trump signed into law a federal spending package.

With the shutdown now over for the time being, investors are hoping that delayed economic reports will soon start to come. However, two major economic reports on jobs and inflation for October may never be released even as the government shutdown ends, according to White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt.

“The gears of the government should be working again soon, and while that is a relief for markets and the economy, there is still plenty of uncertainty, particularly around the missed inflation and jobs data and how these fronts have been faring. While we have always expected that many of the data points missed during the shutdown will remain dark, there are questions about what the inflation and jobs data will look like once these reports come back online,” said Carol Schleif, chief market strategist at BMO Private Wealth.

“We would not be surprised to see some market chop over the coming weeks as the government gears and economic data presses get turning again. The data blackout has made the Federal Reserve's job difficult, but we still expect them to cut interest rates again in December,” Schleif added.

On the earnings side, The Walt Disney Company (DIS) was last -8.8% after the company published its FQ4 earnings report. Revenue dipped 0.5% year-over-year as the Entertainment segment saw revenue decline 6%.

On the other hand, Cisco (CSCO) shares were +4.5% after the networking equipment giant reported fiscal first-quarter results that were above Wall Street's estimates and raised its full-year forecast. The company's stock is near its all-time high.

“Earnings so far have been better than expected, with double-digit growth rates and solid participation across most economic sectors. Better yet, the bottom line is coming in well ahead of strong revenue growth, indicating intense focus on extracting margin improvement,” said Schleif.

“It's becoming clear that earnings expectations have been too low ever since they were cut in the aftermath of Liberation Day in April. That has also helped stocks clear a lower bar and helped to propel stocks to record heights,” she added.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.11%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.60%. Investors are awaiting the results of a $25B, 30-Year Bond Auction.

The Fed's balance sheet will land later in the day.

Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Wednesday, with the Dow Jones reaching a new record high close, while Nasdaq ended in the red, as several big tech stocks lagged.

More on markets:

data.title: Manulife outlines refreshed strategy and targets 18%+ core ROE by 2027 as Asia and global WAM drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Manulife Financial Corporation (MFC:CA) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Philip Witherington, President, CEO & Director, opened with a tribute to Marc Costantini, noting his impact on reinsurance transactions and announcing Naveed Irshad's expanded role as President and CEO of Manulife Canada, now also overseeing Inforce Management and reinsurance globally. Witherington outlined a refreshed enterprise strategy, emphasizing "new and elevated strategic priorities" to drive sustainable growth and build on shareholder return momentum. He highlighted the company's agreement with Mahindra to form a joint venture and enter the India insurance market, stating, "Mahindra is an incredibly strong and trusted partner... and I'm excited about expanding our partnership further."

  • Witherington stressed, "We will also leverage our early leadership in AI to become a truly AI-powered organization," and reiterated a focus on distribution, customer engagement, and maintaining a balanced portfolio. He declared, "Core EPS grew 16% from the prior year supported by a record level of core earnings," and pointed to a "core ROE of 18.1%."

  • Colin Simpson, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "The third quarter was indeed a strong quarter for Manulife, where we continue to demonstrate the ongoing strength, quality and resilience of our business." Simpson detailed, "APE sales increased 8% from the prior year, with strong contributions from our North American businesses," and referenced "net outflows of $6.2 billion for Global WAM following 6 consecutive quarters of positive net flows."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its 2027 targets, with Witherington stating, "We remain confident that we'll deliver on it," regarding the 18%+ core ROE target. He commented on the India joint venture, estimating it will take "12 to 18 months to get this operation off the ground and up and running, including the regulatory approval process."

  • Simpson provided guidance on remittances: "We expect our remittances for 2025 to be approximately $6 billion, putting us well on our way to achieving our cumulative 2027 target of at least $22 billion."

  • Paul Lorentz, President and CEO of Global Wealth & Asset Management, restated, "the guidance we provided around about USD 25 million a quarter remains intact once we get through the entire [eMPF] transition."

Financial Results

  • Core EPS increased 16% from the prior year, reflecting strong double-digit growth in core earnings and the impact of share buybacks. Book value per share rose 7% from the prior year, and the LICAT ratio remained strong at 138%. The leverage ratio stood at 22.7%.

  • Asia delivered a 29% year-on-year increase in core earnings, with NBV margin expanding to 39%. Global WAM reported a record level of core earnings, with its core EBITDA margin expanding 310 basis points year-on-year to 30.9%. Net outflows for Global WAM totaled $6.2 billion, attributed to headwinds in North American Retail and the U.S. retirement channel.

  • In Canada, APE sales rose 9% from the prior year, with group and individual insurance driving growth. The U.S. segment saw APE sales growth of 51%, more than doubling new business CSM, though core earnings decreased 20% year-on-year due to unfavorable claims experience.

  • The company returned over $1.3 billion of capital to shareholders in the quarter, with adjusted book value per share reaching $38.22, representing a 12% increase from the prior year quarter.

Q&A

  • John Aiken, Jefferies, asked about the India JV. Witherington described regulatory and market readiness and the advantages Mahindra brings. He projected a "12 to 18 months" timeline for launch.

  • Taylor Scott, Barclays, asked about Asian market growth and private credit exposure. Steven Finch, President & CEO of Manulife Asia, noted "continued solid momentum in sales growth" and broad-based success in Asia. Trevor Kreel, Chief Investment Officer, provided details on the private credit portfolio, emphasizing its diversity and conservative strategy.

  • Gabriel Dechaine, National Bank, inquired about Global WAM eMPF fee changes and actuarial review impacts. Lorentz detailed expense actions and margin impacts, while Stephanie Fadous, Chief Actuary, clarified that higher utilization is driven by medical cost inflation.

  • Tom MacKinnon, BMO, probed the rationale for the refreshed strategy and capital deployment. Witherington explained, "The logic for the refreshed strategy update... is that given that the external environment continues to evolve, it's really important that the strategy is never static."

  • Doug Young, Desjardins, discussed actuarial methodology changes and credit provision reversals. Fadous and Kreel explained the reserve changes and ECL model impacts.

  • Paul Holden, CIBC, focused on Hong Kong APE sales and strategic growth investments. Finch and Witherington addressed seasonality and investment impacts, with Witherington expecting "low to mid-single digits for the U.S. and a little higher for Canada."

  • Mario Mendonca, TD, questioned the scope of U.S. business growth and efficiency targets. Witherington confirmed no return to higher-risk products and maintained a "sub-45%" efficiency ratio target.

  • Darko Mihelic, RBC, asked about corporate segment CSM and India build-out. Simpson and Finch provided outlooks, with Witherington estimating a "USD 400 million capital injection" over the next decade for India.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was largely constructive but inquisitive, focusing on new market entries, actuarial changes, and the outlook for key segments. There was heightened interest in the India JV and expense management, with some pressing on product expansion and efficiency ratios.

  • Management maintained a confident stance throughout, with Witherington frequently emphasizing "confidence" and "resilience," notably stating, "I'm confident that executing on our strategy will further strengthen our ability to deliver on both our 2027 financial targets and sustain our growth for the next decade and beyond." In Q&A, management addressed challenges directly and reiterated capital discipline.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management presented a more forward-looking and growth-oriented tone, with greater emphasis on strategic expansion, particularly in Asia and India.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the announcement of the India JV, a notable strategic shift absent in the previous quarter, which had focused on the Comvest acquisition. Management reinforced the 18%+ core ROE target and laid out explicit remittance guidance for 2025 and 2027.

  • New business CSM growth was robust in both quarters, but the current quarter highlighted a shift to a "refreshed enterprise strategy" and balanced portfolio growth, including deliberate investments in Canada and the U.S.

  • Analyst focus shifted from acquisition rationale and integration (Comvest in Q2) to new market entries, actuarial assumptions, and the impact of regulatory and macroeconomic changes in Q3.

  • Management’s tone was more optimistic and strategic in Q3, emphasizing long-term growth and diversification, compared to the steadier, more operational focus in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted net outflows in Global WAM as a headwind, referencing "continued pressure in the intermediary and wealth channels" and "anticipated" participant withdrawals in the U.S. retirement business.

  • The actuarial review of the U.S. long-term care business flagged higher utilization due to medical inflation, though the net impact was described as modestly favorable.

  • Credit risk was discussed, with Kreel noting that private credit exposure is "on the low end of the risk spectrum" and that ECL charges could reverse if market conditions deteriorate.

  • Analysts expressed concern about efficiency ratios amid new investments, but management maintained its commitment to the "sub-45%" target, supported by AI-driven productivity.

Final Takeaway

Manulife’s third quarter highlighted a refreshed growth strategy anchored in diversification, digital innovation, and new market expansion, especially through the planned India joint venture with Mahindra. The company reported robust new business growth in Asia and Global WAM, maintained strong profitability metrics, and reaffirmed confidence in its 2027 financial targets and capital return plans. Management emphasized disciplined execution and strategic investments in key markets as critical to delivering long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Diversified Healthcare upgraded at RBC on senior housing tailwinds
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets recently upgraded Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) to Sector Perform from Underperform, in part due to robust senior housing tailwinds and the expectation that leverage will improve markedly.

DHC shares climbed 6.1% in Thursday late morning trading. Earlier in the week, the REIT published mixed headline results for the third quarter, while maintaining its full-year guidance for its senior housing operating portfolio's ("SHOP") net operating income.

Analyst Michael Carroll was particularly "encouraged by the company's ability to transition to 7 new operators to take over the 116 communities previously managed by AlerisLife (reflects ~80% of total SHOP NOI)," he wrote in a note.

"We believe these new operators are better positioned to capitalize on the strong seniors housing backdrop and drive better overall performance," he added.

In addition, the company's leverage is expected to improve over the next 12 to 24 months, the note said, with the net debt/EBITDA ratio estimated to fall to 8.0x in the end of 2026 from 10.0x in Q3 2025. DHC was said to have set an initial target of achieving 6.5x-7.5x.

RBC's Sector Perform rate compares with the SA Quant system rating of Sell, as well as the average SA analyst rating and the average Wall Street analyst rating, both at Hold.

data.title: Blackstone to build $1.2B West Virginia gas power plant
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) confirmed plans Thursday to invest $1.2 billion to build a 600 MW natural gas power generation facility in Harrison County, West Virginia, to help meet rising electricity demand from AI data centers and other energy-intensive industries.

The Wolf Summit project, which would be the first-ever combined-cycle natural gas power plant built in West Virginia, will supply power to Old Dominion Electric Cooperative, which serves ~1.5 million residents across Virginia, Maryland and Delaware, the company said.

Wolf Summit adds to Blackstone's (BX) recent U.S. energy investments, including the Hill Top Energy Center in Pennsylvania and the Potomac Energy Center in Virginia; the firm said its Blackstone Energy Transition Partners unit has backed ~1,600 MW of new power generation in the U.S. during the past three-and-a-half years.

"Helping meet the rising demand for electricity from AI and other areas is among our highest conviction investment themes," Blackstone (BX) senior managing director Bilal Khan said.

data.title: IRS boosts contribution limits for 401(k)s and IRAs in 2026
data.description:
The Internal Revenue Service unveiled on Thursday new limits for contributions people can make to their retirement savings plans in 2026.

The 401((k)) limit increased by $1,000 to $24,500 for next year, while the limit on annual contributions to an IRA (individual retirement account) increased to $7,500 from $7,000.

The IRA catch-up contribution limit for individuals who are at least 50 years old was amended to include an annual cost-of-living adjustment to $1,100 from $1,000 in 2025, the IRS said.

The catch-up contribution limit for 401((k)), governmental 457 plans, and the federal government's Thrift Savings Plan rose to $8,000 for 2026, from $7,500 for 2025. Under a change made in SECURE 2.0 legislation, employees who are aged 60 to 63 can make a higher catch-up contribution. That limit remains at $11,250.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
